title: RedFuser: An Automatic Operator Fusion Framework for Cascaded Reductions on AI Accelerators
summary:
Operator fusion, as a key performance optimization technique in the deployment of AI models, significantly improves execution efficiency and has been widely adopted in modern AI compilers. However, for cascaded reduction operations involving multiple loops with inter-loop data dependencies, such as the safe softmax followed by GEMM within attention mechanisms, existing compilers lack effective automated fusion and kernel generation capabilities. Although some works have addressed specific instances through hand-crafted fusion strategies, their solutions are limited in generality and difficult to extend to other similar structures. Given the prevalence of such computational patterns in deep learning models, there remains significant untapped potential in achieving general and automated fusion optimization.
  In this paper, we present a formal theoretical methodology for analyzing cascaded reductions which can fuse them into a single loop and introduce an incremental computation form. Based on this methodology, we design Reduction Fuser (RedFuser), a framework that automatically identifies supported cascaded reduction patterns and generates optimized fused kernels. Experiments show that RedFuser successfully fuses diverse workloads, achieving up to 2$\times$ to 5$\times$ speedup over state-of-the-art AI compilers and matching the performance of highly optimized hand-written kernels. The code is available at https://github.com/alibaba/redfuser

AI.tldr: RedFuser is an automated framework that fuses cascaded reduction operations (like safe softmax + GEMM in attention) into single loops using incremental computation, achieving 2-5× speedup over existing compilers.
AI.motivation: Existing AI compilers lack effective automated fusion for cascaded reduction operations with inter-loop dependencies, which are common in deep learning models (e.g., attention mechanisms). Current hand-crafted solutions are not generalizable.
AI.method: Developed a formal theoretical methodology to analyze cascaded reductions and fuse them into single loops using incremental computation. Built Reduction Fuser (RedFuser) framework that automatically identifies supported patterns and generates optimized fused kernels.
AI.result: RedFuser successfully fuses diverse workloads, achieving 2× to 5× speedup over state-of-the-art AI compilers and matches performance of highly optimized hand-written kernels.
AI.conclusion: RedFuser provides a general, automated solution for fusing cascaded reduction operations that addresses limitations of existing compilers and hand-crafted approaches, unlocking significant performance optimization potential.

title: The DMA Streaming Framework: Kernel-Level Buffer Orchestration for High-Performance AI Data Paths
summary: AI transport libraries move bytes efficiently, but they commonly assume that buffers are already correctly allocated, placed, shared, registered, and safe under completion and teardown pressure. This paper presents dmaplane, a Linux kernel module that makes this missing layer explicit as buffer orchestration. dmaplane exposes a stable kernel UAPI via /dev/dmaplane and composes ring-based command channels, DMA buffer lifecycle management, dma-buf export for cross-device sharing, a kernel-space RDMA engine, NUMA-aware allocation and verification, credit-based flow control, low-overhead observability, and GPU memory integration via PCIe BAR pinning. We evaluate orchestration sensitivity with measurements of NUMA cross-node penalties at DRAM scale, completion-safe flow control under sustained RDMA load, and GPU BAR mapping tiers versus cudaMemcpy. We also demonstrate end-to-end disaggregated inference by transferring KV-cache chunks between two machines using RDMA WRITE WITH IMMEDIATE and reconstructing tensor views on the receiver. RDMA measurements use Soft-RoCE; we distinguish measured results from provider-independent properties by construction.

AI.tldr: dmaplane is a Linux kernel module that provides buffer orchestration for AI transport, handling allocation, placement, sharing, registration, and safety that existing libraries assume but don't provide.
AI.motivation: Current AI transport libraries efficiently move bytes but assume buffers are already properly allocated, placed, shared, registered, and safe under completion and teardown pressure. There's a missing layer for buffer orchestration that needs to be made explicit.
AI.method: dmaplane is implemented as a Linux kernel module exposing a stable UAPI via /dev/dmaplane. It provides ring-based command channels, DMA buffer lifecycle management, dma-buf export for cross-device sharing, kernel-space RDMA engine, NUMA-aware allocation and verification, credit-based flow control, low-overhead observability, and GPU memory integration via PCIe BAR pinning.
AI.result: The paper evaluates orchestration sensitivity by measuring NUMA cross-node penalties at DRAM scale, completion-safe flow control under sustained RDMA load, and GPU BAR mapping tiers versus cudaMemcpy. It also demonstrates end-to-end disaggregated inference by transferring KV-cache chunks between machines using RDMA WRITE WITH IMMEDIATE and reconstructing tensor views on the receiver.
AI.conclusion: dmaplane makes buffer orchestration explicit as a missing layer in AI transport, providing comprehensive buffer management capabilities that existing libraries assume but don't implement, enabling more robust and efficient data movement in distributed AI systems.

title: Architecture-Aware LLM Inference Optimization on AMD Instinct GPUs: A Comprehensive Benchmark and Deployment Study
summary: We present a cross-architecture evaluation of production LLM inference on AMD Instinct MI325X GPUs, benchmarking four models spanning 235B to 1 trillion parameters across three architectural families (MoE+MLA, Dense+GQA, MoE+GQA) on an 8-GPU cluster with 2TB aggregate HBM3e using vLLM v0.14.1. Our results demonstrate that architecture-aware optimization is essential: MLA models require block size 1 and cannot use KV cache offloading, while GQA models benefit from both. The AMD AITER runtime is required for competitive MLA inference throughput and must be selectively disabled for architectures with incompatible attention head configurations. A controlled AITER ablation on Llama-3.1-405B (n=5 per condition) reveals a modest 3-5% throughput benefit at high concurrency but 2-16x higher measurement variability, confirming that AITER's large speedups target MoE/MLA kernels specifically. Under text-only workloads, Llama-405B and DeepSeek V3.2 achieve comparable peak throughput (15,944 and 15,343 tok/s) despite an order-of-magnitude difference in active parameters. Under vision workloads, Qwen3-VL-235B reaches 47,873 tok/s, 6.5x higher than Kimi-K2.5 (7,327 tok/s). Active parameter count per token is associated with inference throughput, though confounded by differences in quantization, AITER acceleration, and tensor parallelism. All four models exhibit a common throughput saturation point consistent with a memory-bandwidth bottleneck (~500 concurrent for short sequences, ~100-200 for longer sequences). All models maintain 100% HTTP-level success rates through 1,000 concurrent users, processing 18.9 million tokens across 17,406 requests without failures.

AI.tldr: Cross-architecture evaluation of LLM inference on AMD MI325X GPUs shows architecture-aware optimization is crucial, with different models requiring specific configurations for optimal performance.
AI.motivation: To benchmark and evaluate production LLM inference performance across different architectural families on AMD Instinct MI325X GPUs, identifying architecture-specific optimization requirements and performance characteristics.
AI.method: Benchmarked four models (235B to 1T parameters) across three architectural families (MoE+MLA, Dense+GQA, MoE+GQA) on an 8-GPU cluster with 2TB HBM3e using vLLM v0.14.1. Conducted controlled AITER ablation studies and measured performance under text-only and vision workloads with up to 1,000 concurrent users.
AI.result: MLA models require block size 1 and cannot use KV cache offloading, while GQA models benefit from both. AMD AITER runtime is essential for competitive MLA inference throughput. Llama-405B and DeepSeek V3.2 achieved comparable peak throughput (~15k tok/s) despite parameter count differences. Qwen3-VL-235B reached 47,873 tok/s on vision workloads. All models showed throughput saturation consistent with memory-bandwidth bottleneck and maintained 100% success rates through 1,000 concurrent users.
AI.conclusion: Architecture-aware optimization is essential for production LLM inference on AMD GPUs. Different architectural families require specific configurations, and while active parameter count influences throughput, it's confounded by other factors. All tested models demonstrate robust performance under high concurrency with consistent memory-bandwidth bottlenecks.

title: HTM-EAR: Importance-Preserving Tiered Memory with Hybrid Routing under Saturation
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures, 3 tables. Code available at GitHub
summary:
Memory constraints in long-running agents require structured management of accumulated facts while preserving essential information under bounded context limits. We introduce HTM-EAR, a hierarchical tiered memory substrate that integrates HNSW-based working memory (L1) with archival storage (L2), combining importance-aware eviction and hybrid routing. When L1 reaches capacity, items are evicted using a weighted score of importance and usage. Queries are first resolved in L1; if similarity or entity coverage is insufficient, retrieval falls back to L2, and candidates are re-ranked using a cross-encoder.
  We evaluate the system under sustained saturation (15,000 facts; L1 capacity 500; L2 capacity 5000) using synthetic streams across five random seeds and real BGL system logs. Ablation studies compare the full system against variants without cross-encoder re-ranking, without routing gates, with LRU eviction, and an oracle with unbounded memory. Under saturation, the full model preserves active-query precision (MRR = 1.000) while enabling controlled forgetting of stale history, approaching oracle active performance (0.997 +/- 0.003). In contrast, LRU minimizes latency (21.1 ms) but permanently evicts 2416 essential facts. On BGL logs, the full system achieves MRR 0.336, close to the oracle (0.370), while LRU drops to 0.069. Code is publicly available at: https://github.com/shubham-61291/HTM-EAR

AI.tldr: HTM-EAR is a hierarchical memory system for long-running AI agents that manages memory constraints through tiered storage with intelligent eviction and retrieval mechanisms, maintaining high precision while enabling controlled forgetting of stale information.
AI.motivation: Long-running AI agents face memory constraints that require structured management of accumulated facts while preserving essential information under bounded context limits, necessitating a system that can intelligently manage what to keep and what to forget.
AI.method: A hierarchical tiered memory system with HNSW-based working memory (L1) and archival storage (L2), using importance-aware eviction with weighted scores of importance and usage, hybrid routing, and cross-encoder re-ranking for retrieval.
AI.result: Under saturation with 15,000 facts, the full model preserves active-query precision (MRR = 1.000) and approaches oracle performance (0.997 +/- 0.003). On BGL system logs, it achieves MRR 0.336 (close to oracle 0.370), while LRU drops to 0.069 and permanently evicts essential facts.
AI.conclusion: HTM-EAR effectively manages memory constraints in long-running agents by combining hierarchical storage with intelligent eviction and retrieval, maintaining high precision while enabling controlled forgetting, outperforming simple LRU strategies that lose essential information.

title: Early-Stage Cancer Biomarker Detection via Intravascular Nanomachines: Modeling and Analysis
summary: Early detection of cancer is essential for timely diagnosis and improved patient outcomes. Among emerging technologies, intra-body nanoscale communication offers an innovative solution to identify molecular cues within the human bloodstream. This study investigates a minimally invasive approach for early-stage cancer biomarker detection using nanomachines introduced into the bloodstream. To assess the feasibility of this approach, computational simulations are used to emulate the vascular environment and evaluate biomarker detection performance under different physiological conditions. Current modeling approaches often fail to capture essential vascular characteristics, including non-uniform flow structures, size-dependent particle mobility, and particle margination driven by red blood cell interactions. To address these limitations, our study incorporates these factors into the simulation framework and quantifies their individual and combined effects on biomarker detection efficiency. Baseline detection performance is first obtained under uniform flow assumptions, after which introducing realistic vascular transport mechanisms progressively reduces detection probability for all vessel types and nanomachine sizes. Among the considered vessels, capillary consistently achieves the highest detection probability across all nanomachine sizes.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates early cancer detection using nanomachines in bloodstream, focusing on computational modeling of vascular transport mechanisms that affect biomarker detection efficiency.
AI.motivation: Early cancer detection is crucial for improved patient outcomes. Current modeling approaches fail to capture essential vascular characteristics like non-uniform flow, size-dependent particle mobility, and particle margination driven by red blood cell interactions.
AI.method: Computational simulations emulate vascular environment to evaluate biomarker detection performance. The study incorporates realistic vascular transport mechanisms including non-uniform flow structures, size-dependent particle mobility, and particle margination from red blood cell interactions.
AI.result: Baseline detection under uniform flow assumptions shows highest performance, but introducing realistic vascular transport mechanisms progressively reduces detection probability for all vessel types and nanomachine sizes. Capillaries consistently achieve the highest detection probability across all nanomachine sizes.
AI.conclusion: Realistic modeling of vascular transport mechanisms is essential for accurate assessment of nanomachine-based cancer detection systems. Capillaries offer the most promising environment for biomarker detection using intra-body nanoscale communication approaches.

title: RAGPerf: An End-to-End Benchmarking Framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation Systems
comment: The codebase of RAGPerf is available at https://github.com/platformxlab/RAGPerf
summary: We present the design and implementation of a RAG-based AI system benchmarking (RAGPerf) framework for characterizing the system behaviors of RAG pipelines. To facilitate detailed profiling and fine-grained performance analysis, RAGPerf decouples the RAG workflow into several modular components - embedding, indexing, retrieval, reranking, and generation. RAGPerf offers the flexibility for users to configure the core parameters of each component and examine their impact on the end-to-end query performance and quality. RAGPerf has a workload generator to model real-world scenarios by supporting diverse datasets (e.g., text, pdf, code, and audio), different retrieval and update ratios, and query distributions. RAGPerf also supports different embedding models, major vector databases such as LanceDB, Milvus, Qdrant, Chroma, and Elasticsearch, as well as different LLMs for content generation. It automates the collection of performance metrics (i.e., end-to-end query throughput, host/GPU memory footprint, and CPU/GPU utilization) and accuracy metrics (i.e., context recall, query accuracy, and factual consistency). We demonstrate the capabilities of RAGPerf through a comprehensive set of experiments and open source its codebase at GitHub. Our evaluation shows that RAGPerf incurs negligible performance overhead.

AI.tldr: RAGPerf is a benchmarking framework for RAG systems that decouples workflows into modular components, supports various configurations and datasets, and collects performance/accuracy metrics with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: There's a need for systematic benchmarking of RAG-based AI systems to understand their system behaviors, performance characteristics, and quality metrics across different configurations and real-world scenarios.
AI.method: RAGPerf decouples RAG workflows into modular components (embedding, indexing, retrieval, reranking, generation), allows flexible parameter configuration, includes a workload generator for diverse datasets and query distributions, supports various embedding models, vector databases, and LLMs, and automates metric collection.
AI.result: The framework incurs negligible performance overhead and demonstrates capabilities through comprehensive experiments. It's open-sourced on GitHub, enabling detailed profiling and fine-grained performance analysis of RAG pipelines.
AI.conclusion: RAGPerf provides a flexible, comprehensive benchmarking framework for RAG systems that enables systematic evaluation of performance and quality metrics across diverse configurations and real-world scenarios.

title: AgentServe: Algorithm-System Co-Design for Efficient Agentic AI Serving on a Consumer-Grade GPU
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as AI agents that operate in short reasoning-action loops, interleaving model computation with external calls. Unlike traditional chat applications, these agentic workloads require inference serving systems to balance low latency, stable token emission, and throughput under multiple request arrivals from different AI agents. Recent deployments highlight a shift toward running small language models (SLMs) locally on consumer-grade GPUs, driven by privacy, compliance, and cost constraints. When heterogeneous requests overlap on a single GPU, long prefills and short decodes contend for resources, creating head-of-line blocking that destabilizes interactive performance. By analyzing agent workloads, we observe that their execution naturally separates into cold prefills, which process long system prompts, resume prefills, which append tool outputs to cached contexts, and short decodes, which are latency-critical. This mix intensifies contention compared to conventional chatbot serving. We present AgentServe, a single-GPU serving system that ensures stable multi-agent execution under such conditions by isolating prefills from decodes, applying dynamic budgeting to resume prefills, and allocating GPU resources through pre-established CUDA Green Context slots with adaptive control. Evaluation results show that AgentServe significantly improves latency stability while sustaining competitive throughput, achieving up to 2.8x TTFT improvement and 2.7x TPOT improvement over state-of-the-art baselines across different settings.

AI.tldr: AgentServe is a single-GPU serving system for AI agents that isolates prefills from decodes and uses dynamic budgeting to ensure stable multi-agent execution with improved latency stability and throughput.
AI.motivation: AI agents using LLMs require inference serving systems that balance low latency, stable token emission, and throughput. When heterogeneous requests (long prefills and short decodes) overlap on a single GPU, they create head-of-line blocking that destabilizes interactive performance, especially with the shift toward running small language models locally on consumer-grade GPUs for privacy, compliance, and cost reasons.
AI.method: AgentServe isolates prefills from decodes, applies dynamic budgeting to resume prefills, and allocates GPU resources through pre-established CUDA Green Context slots with adaptive control. It addresses the three types of agent workload execution: cold prefills (process long system prompts), resume prefills (append tool outputs to cached contexts), and short decodes (latency-critical).
AI.result: AgentServe significantly improves latency stability while sustaining competitive throughput, achieving up to 2.8x TTFT (Time To First Token) improvement and 2.7x TPOT (Time Per Output Token) improvement over state-of-the-art baselines across different settings.
AI.conclusion: AgentServe effectively addresses the unique challenges of serving AI agent workloads on single GPUs by isolating different types of requests and implementing dynamic resource allocation, providing stable multi-agent execution with improved performance metrics compared to existing solutions.

title: Multi-Agent Memory from a Computer Architecture Perspective: Visions and Challenges Ahead
summary: As LLM agents evolve into collaborative multi-agent systems, their memory requirements grow rapidly in complexity. This position paper frames multi-agent memory as a computer architecture problem. We distinguish shared and distributed memory paradigms, propose a three-layer memory hierarchy (I/O, cache, and memory), and identify two critical protocol gaps: cache sharing across agents and structured memory access control. We argue that the most pressing open challenge is multi-agent memory consistency. Our architectural framing provides a foundation for building reliable, scalable multi-agent systems.

AI.tldr: This position paper frames multi-agent memory as a computer architecture problem, proposing a three-layer memory hierarchy and identifying critical protocol gaps for reliable, scalable multi-agent systems.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents evolve into collaborative multi-agent systems, their memory requirements grow rapidly in complexity, necessitating a systematic architectural approach to memory management.
AI.method: The paper proposes an architectural framing with: 1) distinction between shared and distributed memory paradigms, 2) a three-layer memory hierarchy (I/O, cache, and memory), and 3) identification of critical protocol gaps including cache sharing across agents and structured memory access control.
AI.result: The analysis identifies multi-agent memory consistency as the most pressing open challenge and provides an architectural foundation for building reliable, scalable multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: Framing multi-agent memory as a computer architecture problem provides a systematic foundation for addressing the growing complexity of memory requirements in collaborative multi-agent systems, with memory consistency being the key challenge to solve.

title: Reference Architecture of a Quantum-Centric Supercomputer
summary: Quantum computers have demonstrated utility in simulating quantum systems beyond brute-force classical approaches. As the community builds on these demonstrations to explore using quantum computing for applied research, algorithms and workflows have emerged that require leveraging both quantum computers and classical high-performance computing (HPC) systems to scale applications, especially in chemistry and materials, beyond what either system can simulate alone. Today, these disparate systems operate in isolation, forcing users to manually orchestrate workloads, coordinate job scheduling, and transfer data between systems -- a cumbersome process that hinders productivity and severely limits rapid algorithmic exploration. These challenges motivate the need for flexible and high-performance Quantum-Centric Supercomputing (QCSC) systems that integrate Quantum Processing Units (QPUs), Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), and Central Processing Units (CPUs) to accelerate discovery of such algorithms across applications. These systems will be co-designed across quantum and classical HPC infrastructure, middleware, and application layers to accelerate the adoption of quantum computing for solving critical computational problems. We envision QCSC evolution through three distinct phases: (1) quantum systems as specialized compute offload engines within existing HPC complexes; (2) heterogeneous quantum and classical HPC systems coupled through advanced middleware, enabling seamless execution of hybrid quantum-classical algorithms; and (3) fully co-designed heterogeneous quantum-HPC systems for hybrid computational workflows. This article presents a reference architecture and roadmap for these QCSC systems.

AI.tldr: Quantum-Centric Supercomputing (QCSC) integrates quantum and classical HPC systems to accelerate hybrid quantum-classical algorithms for applied research in chemistry and materials science.
AI.motivation: Current quantum and classical HPC systems operate in isolation, requiring manual orchestration that hinders productivity and limits rapid algorithmic exploration. There's a need for integrated systems to accelerate discovery of quantum algorithms for solving critical computational problems.
AI.method: Proposes a three-phase evolution of QCSC systems: (1) quantum systems as specialized compute offload engines within existing HPC complexes; (2) heterogeneous quantum-classical systems coupled through advanced middleware; (3) fully co-designed heterogeneous quantum-HPC systems for hybrid computational workflows.
AI.result: Presents a reference architecture and roadmap for QCSC systems that integrate Quantum Processing Units (QPUs), Graphics Processing Units (GPUs), and Central Processing Units (CPUs) through co-design across infrastructure, middleware, and application layers.
AI.conclusion: QCSC systems will accelerate the adoption of quantum computing for solving critical computational problems by enabling seamless execution of hybrid quantum-classical algorithms and overcoming current limitations of isolated quantum and classical systems.

title: S-HPLB: Efficient LLM Attention Serving via Sparsity-Aware Head Parallelism Load Balance
summary: With the increasing volumes of Large Language Models (LLMs) and the expanding context lengths, attention computation has become a key performance bottleneck in LLM serving. For fast attention computation, recent practices often parallelize the attention heads on multiple GPUs, and also widely adopt attention sparsification to reduce the computation amount -- which selectively computes a subset of attention pairs under a preset sparsity budget. In this paper, we notice that attention heads of an LLM model often exhibit heterogeneous-yet-stable sparsity elasticities, which motivates us to enforce head-adaptive sparsity budgets to attain better efficiency while preserving high inference quality. Yet, from the system aspect, with heterogeneous sparsity levels, attention computation time on different heads would be inconsistent, yielding cross-GPU resource bubbles under head-parallel deployment. To further minimize such bubbles, we propose a novel attention deployment strategy called Sparsity-aware Head-Parallel Load Balance (S-HPLB). Experiments on long-context benchmark show that, S-HPLB can achieve a $2.88\times$ improvement in average attention computation latency without quality degradation.

AI.tldr: S-HPLB: A sparsity-aware head-parallel load balancing strategy for LLM attention computation that uses head-adaptive sparsity budgets and load balancing to reduce latency by 2.88× without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Attention computation is a key bottleneck in LLM serving due to increasing model sizes and context lengths. Current approaches use head-parallelization on GPUs and attention sparsification, but don't account for heterogeneous sparsity elasticities across attention heads, leading to inefficient resource utilization.
AI.method: Proposes Sparsity-aware Head-Parallel Load Balance (S-HPLB): 1) Enforces head-adaptive sparsity budgets based on each head's sparsity elasticity, 2) Implements load balancing to minimize cross-GPU resource bubbles caused by heterogeneous computation times across heads.
AI.result: Achieves 2.88× improvement in average attention computation latency on long-context benchmarks without quality degradation compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: S-HPLB effectively addresses the attention computation bottleneck in LLM serving by leveraging head-adaptive sparsity and intelligent load balancing, significantly improving efficiency while maintaining inference quality.

title: Pooling Engram Conditional Memory in Large Language Models using CXL
summary: Engram conditional memory has emerged as a promising component for LLMs by decoupling static knowledge lookup from dynamic computation. Since Engram exhibits sparse access patterns and supports prefetching, its massive embedding tables are well-suited for offloading to lower-tier memory. In this paper, we propose using Compute Express Link (CXL) memory pool for Engram storage. Compared to RDMA, CXL provides fine-grained and low-latency access required by minimal and discrete retrieval patterns of Engram. We integrate the CXL-based Engram pool into SGLang, achieving near-DRAM end-to-end performance. This provides a scalable and cost-efficient storage solution for future Engram-integrated LLMs without compromising inference performance.

AI.tldr: Using CXL memory pool for Engram storage in LLMs provides near-DRAM performance with cost-effective scaling.
AI.motivation: Engram memory in LLMs has sparse access patterns suitable for offloading to lower-tier memory, but needs fine-grained low-latency access that RDMA doesn't provide well.
AI.method: Proposed using Compute Express Link (CXL) memory pool for Engram storage, integrated into SGLang framework, leveraging CXL's fine-grained low-latency access for Engram's discrete retrieval patterns.
AI.result: Achieved near-DRAM end-to-end performance with CXL-based Engram pool, providing scalable and cost-efficient storage solution.
AI.conclusion: CXL memory pool is an effective storage solution for Engram-integrated LLMs that maintains inference performance while enabling cost-effective scaling.

title: Report for NSF Workshop on Algorithm-Hardware Co-design for Medical Applications
summary: This report summarizes the discussions and recommendations from the NSF Workshop on Algorithm-Hardware Co-design for Medical Applications, held on September 26-27, 2024, in Pittsburgh, PA. The workshop assembled an interdisciplinary cohort of researchers, clinicians, and industry leaders to examine foundational challenges and develop a strategic roadmap for algorithm-hardware co-design in medical computing. The workshop focuses on four thematic areas: (1) teleoperations, telehealth, and surgical operations; (2) wearable and implantable medicine, including implantable living pharmacies; (3) home ICU, hospital systems, and elderly care; and (4) medical sensing, imaging, and reconstruction. This report calls for a fundamental shift in how next-generation medical technologies are conceived, designed, validated, and translated into practice. The report recommends that NSF sustain investment in shared standardized data infrastructures and compute infrastructures, develop clinic workflow-aware systems and human-AI collaboration frameworks, promote scalable validation ecosystems grounded in objective, continuous measures, and physics-informed, and enable safe, accountable, and resilient platforms, including virtual-physical healthcare ecosystems, to de-risk translational pathways. The workshop information can be found on the website: https://sites.google.com/view/nsfworkshop.

AI.tldr: NSF workshop report on algorithm-hardware co-design for medical applications, outlining four thematic areas and recommendations for future research and infrastructure development.
AI.motivation: To address foundational challenges in medical computing by bringing together interdisciplinary experts to develop a strategic roadmap for algorithm-hardware co-design, recognizing the need for integrated approaches in next-generation medical technologies.
AI.method: Conducted an NSF workshop with researchers, clinicians, and industry leaders to examine challenges and develop recommendations through interdisciplinary discussions focused on four thematic areas of medical applications.
AI.result: Identified four key thematic areas requiring algorithm-hardware co-design: (1) teleoperations/telehealth/surgical operations, (2) wearable/implantable medicine, (3) home ICU/hospital systems/elderly care, and (4) medical sensing/imaging/reconstruction. Developed strategic recommendations for NSF investment and infrastructure development.
AI.conclusion: Calls for fundamental shift in medical technology development approach, recommending sustained NSF investment in standardized data/compute infrastructures, clinic workflow-aware systems, scalable validation ecosystems, and safe/resilient platforms to de-risk translational pathways for algorithm-hardware co-designed medical technologies.

title: CD-Raft: Reducing the Latency of Distributed Consensus in Cross-Domain Sites
summary: Today's massive AI computation loads push heavy data synchronization across sites, i.e., nodes in data centers. Any reduction in such consensus latency can significantly improve the overall performance of desired systems. This consensus challenge explosively peaks at cross-domain sites. In this paper, we proposed CD-Raft to address the cross-domain latency challenge, an optimized Raft protocol for strong consistency in cross-domain sites. CD-Raft can significantly reduce consensus latency by optimizing cross-domain round-trip time (RTT) for reads and writes, as well as carefully positioning the leader node. We verified the correctness of CD-Raft in a formal specification using the TLA+ specification, guaranteeing the strong consistency across sites. We have prototyped CD-Raft and evaluated it using the YCSB benchmark. Empirical results show that compared to the classic Raft, CD-Raft reduces the average latency by 32.90% and (99th percentile) tail latency by 49.24% for renown traces across multiple sites.

AI.tldr: CD-Raft is an optimized Raft protocol that reduces consensus latency in cross-domain environments by optimizing cross-domain RTT and leader positioning, achieving 32.90% lower average latency and 49.24% lower tail latency compared to classic Raft.
AI.motivation: Massive AI computation loads create heavy data synchronization across sites in data centers, with consensus latency becoming a critical bottleneck that peaks explosively at cross-domain sites, significantly impacting overall system performance.
AI.method: CD-Raft optimizes the classic Raft protocol for cross-domain environments by reducing cross-domain round-trip time (RTT) for both reads and writes, and carefully positioning the leader node. The correctness is formally verified using TLA+ specification to guarantee strong consistency across sites.
AI.result: Evaluation using YCSB benchmark shows CD-Raft reduces average latency by 32.90% and 99th percentile tail latency by 49.24% compared to classic Raft for renown traces across multiple sites.
AI.conclusion: CD-Raft effectively addresses the cross-domain consensus latency challenge, significantly improving performance for AI workloads and other applications requiring strong consistency across distributed sites.

title: In-Memory ADC-Based Nonlinear Activation Quantization for Efficient In-Memory Computing
summary: In deep networks, operations such as ReLU and hardware-driven clamping often cause activations to accumulate near the edges of the distribution, leading to biased clustering and suboptimal quantization in existing nonlinear (NL) quantization methods. This paper introduces Boundary Suppressed K-Means Quantization (BS-KMQ), a novel NL quantization approach designed to reduce the resolution requirements of analog-to-digital converters (ADCs) in in-memory computing (IMC) systems. By suppressing boundary outliers before clustering, BS-KMQ achieves more balanced and informative NL quantization levels. The resulting NL references are implemented using a reconfigurable in-memory NL-ADC, achieving a 7x area improvement over prior NL-ADC designs. When evaluated on ResNet-18, VGG-16, Inception-V3, and DistilBERT, BS-KMQ achieves at least 3x lower quantization error compared to linear, Lloyd-Max, cumulative distribution function (CDF), and K-means methods. It also improves post-training quantization accuracy by up to 66.8%, 25.4%, 66.6%, and 67.7%, respectively, compared to linear quantization. After low-bit fine-tuning, BS-KMQ maintains competitive accuracy with significantly fewer NL-ADC levels (3/3/4/4b). System-level simulations on ResNet-18 (6/2/3b) demonstrate up to a 4x speedup and 24x energy efficiency improvement over existing IMC accelerators.

AI.tldr: BS-KMQ is a nonlinear quantization method that suppresses boundary outliers before clustering to achieve more balanced quantization levels, reducing ADC resolution requirements in in-memory computing systems with significant improvements in area, speed, and energy efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing nonlinear quantization methods suffer from biased clustering and suboptimal quantization due to activations accumulating near distribution edges from operations like ReLU and hardware clamping, leading to inefficient analog-to-digital converter requirements in in-memory computing systems.
AI.method: Boundary Suppressed K-Means Quantization (BS-KMQ) suppresses boundary outliers before clustering to achieve more balanced and informative nonlinear quantization levels, implemented using a reconfigurable in-memory nonlinear ADC.
AI.result: BS-KMQ achieves 7x area improvement over prior NL-ADC designs, at least 3x lower quantization error compared to linear, Lloyd-Max, CDF, and K-means methods, and improves post-training quantization accuracy by up to 66.8% on various models. After low-bit fine-tuning, it maintains competitive accuracy with fewer NL-ADC levels (3-4 bits). System-level simulations show up to 4x speedup and 24x energy efficiency improvement.
AI.conclusion: BS-KMQ effectively addresses boundary clustering issues in nonlinear quantization, enabling significant reductions in ADC resolution requirements while improving performance metrics for in-memory computing accelerators across various neural network architectures.

title: Double-Precision Matrix Multiplication Emulation via Ozaki-II Scheme with FP8 Quantization
summary: In high-performance computing (HPC) applications, FP64 arithmetic remains indispensable for ensuring numerical accuracy and stability. However, in recent hardware generations, improvements in FP64 arithmetic performance have been relatively modest. Consequently, achieving sustained performance gains for FP64 computations necessitates the effective utilization of high-throughput low-precision arithmetic, such as INT8 and FP8. In several recent architectures, such as NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra and NVIDIA Rubin, INT8 performance has been significantly reduced, making reliance on INT8 alone insufficient. The use of FP8 arithmetic is thus increasingly important. In this paper, we propose a method for emulating double-precision (FP64) general matrix--matrix multiplication (DGEMM), a fundamental and performance-critical kernel in many HPC applications, using FP8 matrix multiply-accumulate (MMA) units. The Ozaki-I and Ozaki-II schemes are well established as foundational approaches for emulating DGEMM via low-precision arithmetic. For DGEMM emulation via the Ozaki-I scheme, implementations using INT8, FP8, and FP16 MMA units have been proposed, all of which can be realized based on the same underlying algorithmic structure. In contrast, although implementations of DGEMM emulation via the Ozaki-II scheme using INT8 MMA units have been reported, the original algorithm cannot be directly adapted to exploit FP8 MMA units. In this work, we introduce a novel technique to overcome this limitation and demonstrate FP64 matrix multiplication emulation based on the Ozaki-II scheme that operates on FP8 MMA units. Compared to FP8-based emulation via the Ozaki-I scheme, our method significantly reduces the number of required FP8 matrix multiplications and enables efficient FP64 emulation on emerging GPU architectures.

AI.tldr: Proposes FP64 DGEMM emulation using FP8 MMA units via modified Ozaki-II scheme, reducing FP8 matrix multiplications compared to Ozaki-I approach.
AI.motivation: FP64 arithmetic is essential for HPC numerical accuracy but hardware improvements are modest. Recent architectures reduce INT8 performance, making FP8 increasingly important for achieving performance gains through low-precision arithmetic.
AI.method: Novel technique to adapt Ozaki-II scheme for FP8 MMA units, overcoming limitations of original algorithm. Enables FP64 matrix multiplication emulation using FP8 hardware units.
AI.result: Significantly reduces number of required FP8 matrix multiplications compared to FP8-based emulation via Ozaki-I scheme. Enables efficient FP64 emulation on emerging GPU architectures.
AI.conclusion: Proposed method provides efficient FP64 DGEMM emulation using FP8 MMA units via modified Ozaki-II scheme, addressing hardware limitations and enabling performance gains in HPC applications.

title: An FPGA Implementation of Displacement Vector Search for Intra Pattern Copy in JPEG XS
summary: Recently, progress has been made on the Intra Pattern Copy (IPC) tool for JPEG XS, an image compression standard designed for low-latency and low-complexity coding. IPC performs wavelet-domain intra compensation predictions to reduce spatial redundancy in screen content. A key module of IPC is the displacement vector (DV) search, which aims to solve the optimal prediction reference offset. However, the DV search process is computationally intensive, posing challenges for practical hardware deployment. In this paper, we propose an efficient pipelined FPGA architecture design for the DV search module to promote the practical deployment of IPC. Optimized memory organization, which leverages the IPC computational characteristics and data inherent reuse patterns, is further introduced to enhance the performance. Experimental results show that our proposed architecture achieves a throughput of 38.3 Mpixels/s with a power consumption of 277 mW, demonstrating its feasibility for practical hardware implementation in IPC and other predictive coding tools, and providing a promising foundation for ASIC deployment.

AI.tldr: Efficient FPGA architecture design for displacement vector search in JPEG XS IPC tool, achieving 38.3 Mpixels/s throughput with 277 mW power consumption.
AI.motivation: The displacement vector (DV) search in JPEG XS IPC tool is computationally intensive, posing challenges for practical hardware deployment. Current implementations need optimization for efficient hardware implementation.
AI.method: Proposed an efficient pipelined FPGA architecture design for the DV search module with optimized memory organization that leverages IPC computational characteristics and data reuse patterns.
AI.result: The proposed architecture achieves throughput of 38.3 Mpixels/s with power consumption of 277 mW, demonstrating feasibility for practical hardware implementation.
AI.conclusion: The efficient FPGA architecture provides a promising foundation for ASIC deployment and promotes practical deployment of IPC and other predictive coding tools in JPEG XS standard.

title: Topological Analysis for Identifying Anomalies in Serverless Platforms
summary: The information flows in serverless platforms are complex and non-conservative. This is a direct result of how independently deployed functions interact under the platform coarse-grained control mechanisms. To manage this complexity, we introduce a topological model for serverless services. Using Hodge decomposition, we can separate observed operational flows into two distinct categories. They include components that can be corrected locally and harmonic modes that persist at any scale. Our analysis reveals that these harmonic flows emerge naturally from different types of inter-function interactions. They should be understood as structural properties of serverless systems, not as configuration errors. Building on this insight, we present an iterative method for analyzing inter-function flows. This method helps deriving practical remediation strategies. One such strategy is the introduction of "dumping effects" to contain harmonic inefficiencies, offering an alternative to completely restructuring the service's topological model. Our experimental results confirm that this approach can uncover latent architectural structures.

AI.tldr: A topological model using Hodge decomposition separates serverless flows into local correctable components and persistent harmonic modes, revealing them as structural properties rather than errors, with practical remediation strategies like "dumping effects."
AI.motivation: Serverless platforms have complex, non-conservative information flows due to independent function interactions under coarse-grained control mechanisms, requiring better management of this complexity.
AI.method: Introduce a topological model for serverless services using Hodge decomposition to separate operational flows into locally correctable components and harmonic modes, plus an iterative method for analyzing inter-function flows.
AI.result: Analysis reveals harmonic flows emerge naturally from inter-function interactions as structural properties, not configuration errors. Experimental results confirm the approach can uncover latent architectural structures.
AI.conclusion: Harmonic inefficiencies in serverless systems can be contained using "dumping effects" as an alternative to complete restructuring, providing practical remediation strategies based on topological analysis.

title: Data Augmentation and Convolutional Network Architecture Influence on Distributed Learning
summary: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have proven to be highly effective in solving a broad spectrum of computer vision tasks, such as classification, identification, and segmentation. These methods can be deployed in both centralized and distributed environments, depending on the computational demands of the task. While much of the literature has focused on the explainability of CNNs, which is essential for building trust and confidence in their predictions, there remains a gap in understanding their impact on computational resources, particularly in distributed training contexts. In this study, we analyze how CNN architectures primarily influence model accuracy and investigate additional factors that affect computational efficiency in distributed systems. Our findings contribute valuable insights for optimizing the deployment of CNNs in resource-intensive scenarios, paving the way for further exploration of variables critical to distributed learning.

AI.tldr: Analysis of how CNN architectures affect accuracy and computational efficiency in distributed training systems.
AI.motivation: While CNN explainability has been well-studied, there's a gap in understanding their computational resource impact in distributed training contexts, which is crucial for optimizing deployment in resource-intensive scenarios.
AI.method: Analyzed how CNN architectures primarily influence model accuracy and investigated additional factors affecting computational efficiency in distributed systems.
AI.result: Findings provide valuable insights for optimizing CNN deployment in resource-intensive scenarios and identify variables critical to distributed learning.
AI.conclusion: The study contributes to understanding computational efficiency factors in distributed CNN training, paving the way for further exploration of optimization variables in distributed learning systems.
